clean_text: Collections Composite data structures are essential for programmers to store and manipulate related data together The most common composite data structures is the array While a string is also a composite data structure, most programming languages treat it as a basic data type and provide native support for manipulation through common operators such as = for value assignment and + for string concatenation In OOP, a composite data structures must group together a set of individual objects and represent them as a single entity In Java, such a group of objects manipulated as a single entity is called a collection Java language environment (JDK) provides a Collections Framework that hold together several classes and interfaces of the type collection Quiz: are you able to differentiate between Java, JVM and JDK?

clean_text: Collections vs ADTs There are familiar composite data structures behaving as collections called Abstract Data Types (ADT) Main among these are the List, Stack (LIFO), Queue (FIFO), Tree, and Table Each of these ADTs have unique interfaces (methods) that clearly identify the specific ADT through its behaviour insert(position, value) and remove(position) for List push(value) and pop() for Stack insert(value) and remove() for Queue search(key) and insert(key,value) for Tree lookup(key) and update(key,value) for Table While ADTs are useful, not having a common interface for manipulating these collections was a disadvantage Different ADTs performing the same task (for example, storing a value) had different method calls

clean_text: Java pre- and post- Collections Object-oriented program designs can benefit from a framework of classes providing the functionality of a collection if they all perform similar tasks in a similar manner Originally, JDK provided the ability to group objects through Arrays (as a native language feature) and Vectors or Hashtables (as utility classes) These classes did not provide a common interface For example, Vector had addElement(value) method while Hashtable had put(key,value) method to insert a value to respective collections In the Java Collections Framework, there are 2 main root interface classes: 1. Collection interface (java.util.Collection) 2. Map interface (java.util.Map)
